name: venv
context: Create and manage Python virtual environments using the stdlib venv module for dependency isolation and reproducible projects
info: The venv module (stdlib since Python 3.3) creates lightweight virtual environments with their own site-packages directory, isolated from system Python. Each venv gets its own python binary (symlink), pip, and site-packages. This is the standard mechanism for isolating project dependencies — every Python project should use one. Create with `python -m venv .venv`, activate with `source .venv/bin/activate` (Linux/macOS) or `.venv\Scripts\activate` (Windows), deactivate with `deactivate`. The venv writes a `pyvenv.cfg` file at its root and sets the `VIRTUAL_ENV` environment variable when active. For the older, more featureful third-party alternative, see virtualenv. For package installation inside a venv, see [[python/pip]]. For higher-level workflow tools that manage venvs automatically, see [[python/poetry]] and [[python/uv]].
body:
# venv — Python Virtual Environments

The `venv` module creates isolated Python environments so each project gets its own dependencies.

## Create

```bash
# Standard creation — .venv is the conventional directory name
python -m venv .venv

# With upgraded pip and setuptools inside the venv
python -m venv --upgrade-deps .venv

# Include system-wide packages (useful for large packages like numpy already installed globally)
python -m venv --system-site-packages .venv

# Minimal venv without pip (for environments using external package managers)
python -m venv --without-pip .venv

# Wipe and recreate an existing venv from scratch
python -m venv --clear .venv
```

## Activate

```bash
# Linux / macOS
source .venv/bin/activate

# Windows (cmd)
.venv\Scripts\activate.bat

# Windows (PowerShell)
.venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1

# Fish shell
source .venv/bin/activate.fish
```

Once activated, your shell prompt changes to `(.venv)` and `python` / `pip` resolve to the venv copies.

```bash
# Leave the virtual environment
deactivate
```

## Running Without Activating

You can run the venv's Python directly without activating:

```bash
.venv/bin/python script.py
.venv/bin/pip install requests
```

## Internals

A venv is a directory with this structure:

```
.venv/
  pyvenv.cfg              # Config: home, include-system-site-packages, version
  bin/                    # Linux/macOS (Scripts/ on Windows)
    python -> /usr/bin/python3  # Symlink to base Python
    pip
    activate              # Shell activation script
  lib/
    python3.x/
      site-packages/      # Installed packages go here
  include/                # C headers for building extensions
```

`pyvenv.cfg` contents:

```ini
home = /usr/bin
include-system-site-packages = false
version = 3.12.0
executable = /usr/bin/python3.12
command = /usr/bin/python3.12 -m venv .venv
```

When activated, the `VIRTUAL_ENV` environment variable points to the venv root and `PATH` is modified to prioritize the venv's `bin/` directory.

## Best Practices

1. **Always use `.venv`** as the directory name — it is the de facto convention
2. **Always add to `.gitignore`:**

```gitignore
.venv/
venv/
env/
```

3. **Create per-project** — one venv per project, inside the project root
4. **Use `--upgrade-deps`** to start with current pip
5. **Pin your Python version** — venvs are tied to the Python that created them
6. **Recreate, don't repair** — if a venv breaks, `--clear` and rebuild from `requirements.txt`
7. **Never commit the venv** — it contains platform-specific binaries

## Integration with Package Managers

After creating and activating a venv:

```bash
# pip — install packages directly
pip install -r requirements.txt

# poetry — creates/manages venvs automatically
poetry install
poetry config virtualenvs.in-project true  # use .venv in project root

# uv — ultrafast pip replacement, venv-aware
uv venv .venv        # uv can also create venvs
uv pip install -r requirements.txt
```

See [[python/pip]], [[python/poetry]], [[python/uv]] for details.

## virtualenv vs venv

| Feature | venv (stdlib) | virtualenv (third-party) |
|---------|--------------|-------------------------|
| Installation | Built-in since 3.3 | `pip install virtualenv` |
| Speed | Standard | Faster (caches seed packages) |
| Python versions | Only the running version | Can target other installed versions |
| Features | Minimal, sufficient | Plugins, config files, more flags |
| Recommendation | Default choice | Use if you need extra features |

Prefer `venv` unless you specifically need virtualenv's features.

sections.title: Creating virtual environments
sections.body: Use `python -m venv .venv` to create a venv. Key flags: `--upgrade-deps` (upgrade pip/setuptools), `--system-site-packages` (inherit system packages), `--clear` (recreate from scratch), `--without-pip` (minimal, no pip). The `.venv` directory name is the standard convention. Always use the specific Python version you want: `python3.12 -m venv .venv`.

sections.title: Activating and deactivating
sections.body: Activate with `source .venv/bin/activate` (Linux/macOS) or `.venv\Scripts\activate` (Windows). Activation sets `VIRTUAL_ENV`, modifies `PATH`, and changes the shell prompt. Deactivate with `deactivate`. You can also run venv binaries directly without activating: `.venv/bin/python script.py`.

sections.title: How venvs work internally
sections.body: A venv directory contains `pyvenv.cfg` (configuration), `bin/` or `Scripts/` (executables and activation scripts), `lib/pythonX.Y/site-packages/` (installed packages), and `include/` (C headers). The `bin/python` is a symlink to the base interpreter. `pyvenv.cfg` records the home directory, Python version, and whether system site-packages are included. The `VIRTUAL_ENV` environment variable is set on activation.

sections.title: Best practices
sections.body: Use `.venv` as the directory name. Add `.venv/`, `venv/`, and `env/` to `.gitignore`. Create one venv per project. Use `--upgrade-deps` for a current pip. Recreate with `--clear` instead of repairing. Never commit the venv directory. Pin your Python version since venvs are tied to the creating interpreter.

scripts.name: Create and Activate Venv
scripts.description: Create a Python virtual environment, activate it, and upgrade pip to latest
scripts.inline:
#!/bin/bash
set -e

PYTHON_PATH="${PYTHON_PATH:-python3}"
VENV_DIR="${VENV_DIR:-.venv}"

echo "Creating venv with: $PYTHON_PATH -m venv $VENV_DIR"
"$PYTHON_PATH" -m venv --upgrade-deps "$VENV_DIR"

echo "Activating venv..."
source "$VENV_DIR/bin/activate"

PYTHON_VERSION=$(python --version 2>&1)
VENV_PATH=$(cd "$VENV_DIR" && pwd)

echo ""
echo "Virtual environment ready:"
echo "  venv_path: $VENV_PATH"
echo "  python_version: $PYTHON_VERSION"
echo "  python: $(which python)"
echo "  pip: $(pip --version)"
echo ""
echo "To activate manually: source $VENV_DIR/bin/activate"

PYTHON_PATH.description: Path to the Python interpreter to use

VENV_DIR.description: Directory name for the virtual environment

python_path.description: Path to the Python interpreter to use for creating the venv

venv_dir.description: Directory name or path for the virtual environment

venv_path.description: Absolute path to the created virtual environment

python_version.description: Python version inside the venv (e.g., Python 3.12.0)

author: dojo-community
